_note: ORIGINAL content. Topic coverage follows the curriculum in Runestone Foundations of Python Programming, Think Python, and OpenStax Introduction to Python Programming. No prose, examples, or exercises were copied; the sources guide topic selection only.
title: Exceptions
summary: Respond to expected failures while leaving unexpected bugs visible.
refresher: Put an operation that may fail inside `try`, catch a specific exception with `except`, and place cleanup in `finally` when both outcomes need it. Avoid a bare `except`, because it can hide mistakes unrelated to the expected problem.

blocks.body: An exception is Python reporting that it cannot finish an operation. Some exceptions come from expected input, such as letters where a number is required. Others expose a coding mistake. Good handling separates those cases.

blocks.caption: Only conversion is protected, and only ValueError is handled. The response matches the failure the program understands.
blocks.output: Credits must be a whole number

blocks.body: When a statement raises, Python skips the rest of the try block and searches for a matching except clause. Code after the whole structure continues unless the handler raises another exception or exits.

blocks.caption: A broad handler can make an unrelated NameError look like bad input. Catching ValueError keeps other bugs honest and visible.

blocks.title: Do not silence evidence
blocks.body: An empty `except` lets the program continue without completing its work or explaining why. Handle the expected case, log useful context, or allow the exception to reach a layer that can respond.

blocks.why: The argument is a string, which is an accepted input type, but its contents do not represent a whole integer. That makes the value invalid.

blocks.body: An `else` block runs only when try succeeds. A `finally` block runs after success or failure, so it belongs to cleanup that must happen on either path. Context managers often handle file cleanup more neatly.

blocks.prompt: Which block runs after try whether an exception occurred or not?
blocks.why: `finally` is reserved for work required on both paths. An except block needs a matching failure, while else requires success.

blocks.title: Exception checklist
items: Protect the smallest operation that can fail.
Catch the most specific useful exception.
Give the caller or user a recovery path.
Use `finally` only for cleanup shared by both outcomes.
